title: Material Editor, Niagara VFX & a Match-3 / Rhythm Game
html:
Surfaces and sparkle

Two systems give Unreal games their look and feel: the Material editor (how surfaces respond to light) and Niagara (how particles bring motion and juice). A match-3 puzzle lives or dies on feedback — a gem clearing should pop, glow, and burst. So we will learn both, then wire them into a grid game and a rhythm game.

PBR materials

Unreal uses physically based rendering. A Material is a node graph that outputs values into the shader's inputs: Base Color, Metallic, Roughness, Normal, Emissive Color, and Opacity. Create one in the Content Browser (Add > Material), double-click to open the graph, and drag wires into the result node.

The most useful early skill is Material Instances. Promote a constant to a parameter (right-click a node, Convert to Parameter), then create an Instance to tweak values per-object without recompiling. This is how one gem material drives six colors.

  • ScalarParameter — a single float, e.g. glow strength.
  • VectorParameter — an RGBA color, e.g. gem tint.
  • Emissive — multiply Base Color by a scalar over 1.0 to make it bloom under Lumen.
Tip.
Parameters set from Blueprint use Create Dynamic Material Instance on a mesh component, then Set Vector Parameter Value. Dynamic instances are how you animate color at runtime.

Driving a material from Blueprint

Inside a gem actor's BeginPlay, build the dynamic instance once and cache it.

BP_Gem (pseudocode)
// Event BeginPlay DynMat = Mesh->CreateDynamicMaterialInstance(0) DynMat->SetVectorParameterValue("Tint", GemColor) // On match, before destroy DynMat->SetScalarParameterValue("Glow", 8.0) SpawnSystemAtLocation(NS_GemBurst, GetActorLocation())

Niagara basics

A Niagara System contains one or more Emitters. Each emitter has stacks of modules grouped by stage: Emitter Spawn, Emitter Update, Particle Spawn, Particle Update, and a Render module. For a gem burst start from the Fountain template, then set Spawn Burst Instantaneous to ~30, add Initial Velocity outward, gravity in Particle Update, and a Color over Life curve fading to zero alpha.

Spawn a system from C++ to prove the wiring:

Gem.cpp
#include "NiagaraFunctionLibrary.h" // add "Niagara" to PublicDependencyModuleNames in Build.cs void AGem::Clear() { UNiagaraFunctionLibrary::SpawnSystemAtLocation( GetWorld(), BurstFX, GetActorLocation()); Destroy(); }
Lab.
Make a single master gem material with a Tint vector and a Glow scalar. Create six instances by color. Confirm setting Glow to 6 makes the gem bloom in a Lumen scene.

The match-3 grid

Model the board as a 2D structure. In Blueprint a Map of IntPoint to Gem works; in C++ a TArray indexed by y * Width + x is simplest. Detecting a match means scanning rows and columns for three-plus of the same color.

match scan (C++)
int32 Idx(int32 x, int32 y) { return y * Width + x; } // horizontal run-length per row for (int32 y = 0; y < Height; ++y) { int32 run = 1; for (int32 x = 1; x < Width; ++x) { if (Grid[Idx(x,y)] == Grid[Idx(x-1,y)]) run++; else run = 1; if (run >= 3) MarkRun(x, y, run, true); } }

On a valid swap, mark matched cells, clear them (spawn the burst, set glow, destroy), then collapse columns and refill from the top. Animate falls with a Timeline so it reads as gravity, not a snap.

Law of juice.
Every state change deserves feedback. A silent clear feels broken; the same clear with a glow flash, particle burst, and a rising score number feels great. Same logic, very different game.

From grid to rhythm

A rhythm game is a match-3 against time instead of space. Notes scroll toward a hit line; the player presses on beat. Reuse Enhanced Input from earlier lessons. Score by the absolute time delta between the note's target time and the press time, bucketed into Perfect / Good / Miss windows.

🎮 Build This: Puzzle / Rhythm + VFX — Gem Cascade & Beat Lane

This starts a new genre: a match-3 puzzle plus a rhythm mini-game, both built around materials and Niagara juice (no prior game reused).

  1. Build a master gem material with Tint and Glow parameters; make six color instances.
  2. Spawn an 8x8 grid of BP_Gem actors, each caching a dynamic material instance.
  3. Implement click-and-swap input; reject swaps that produce no match.
  4. Scan rows and columns for runs of three or more; mark matched cells.
  5. Clear matches: flash glow, spawn NS_GemBurst Niagara, destroy, add score.
  6. Collapse columns and refill from the top using a Timeline for falling.
  7. Rhythm mode: scroll note actors toward a hit line; map a press action; score by time delta into Perfect/Good/Miss with a particle pop on Perfect.

title: Lumen, Post-Process & a Procedural Endless Runner
html:
Light that just works

UE5's headline feature is Lumen, a fully dynamic global illumination and reflection system. No baking lightmaps, no waiting on builds — move a light and bounced light updates in real time. Pair Lumen with a Post Process Volume for exposure, bloom, and color grading, and even a simple level looks cinematic. We will light a scene, then procedurally stream track chunks to build an endless runner.

Enabling and tuning Lumen

New UE5 projects enable Lumen by default. Confirm under Project Settings > Rendering: Dynamic Global Illumination Method = Lumen and Reflection Method = Lumen. The two pieces that drive look are:

  • A Directional Light with Mobility = Movable — your sun.
  • A SkyLight set to Real Time Capture so ambient light reflects the sky and bounces.
  • A Sky Atmosphere actor for physically based sky color.
Tip.
If surfaces look flat or unlit indoors, add more bounce by raising the directional light intensity and ensuring meshes have Generate Lightmap UVs off-screen issues aside — Lumen uses surface cache, so very small or thin meshes may need Lumen Scene Detail raised in the Post Process Volume.

Post Process Volumes

Drop a Post Process Volume and check Infinite Extent (Unbound) so it affects the whole level. Key settings:

SettingUse
Exposure (Metering)Set to Manual for consistent brightness; auto-exposure can wash out runners.
BloomSells emissive materials; keep intensity subtle (~0.5).
Color GradingSaturation and contrast for mood — push toward stylized.
VignetteFocuses attention on the player's lane.

Driving exposure from C++

You can fade post-process at runtime, e.g. a speed-up flash.

RunnerCamera.cpp
// PPSettings is a FPostProcessSettings on a UCameraComponent void ARunnerPawn::SpeedFlash(float Amount) { auto& PP = Camera->PostProcessSettings; PP.bOverride_BloomIntensity = true; PP.BloomIntensity = FMath::Lerp(0.5f, 2.0f, Amount); PP.bOverride_VignetteIntensity = true; PP.VignetteIntensity = FMath::Lerp(0.4f, 0.9f, Amount); }

Procedural track streaming

An endless runner never loads a finite level. Instead you keep a queue of track chunks — each a Blueprint actor containing geometry, obstacles, and a SceneComponent named Attach at its far end marking where the next chunk snaps on. The flow:

  1. Spawn N starting chunks end to end.
  2. Each frame, when the player passes a chunk, spawn a new one at the last chunk's attach point and recycle the oldest.
TrackManager.cpp
void ATrackManager::SpawnChunk() { FTransform T = NextAttach; int32 Pick = FMath::RandRange(0, ChunkClasses.Num()-1); auto* C = GetWorld()->SpawnActor<ATrackChunk>( ChunkClasses[Pick], T); Chunks.Add(C); NextAttach = C->Attach->GetComponentTransform(); if (Chunks.Num() > MaxLive) { Chunks[0]->Destroy(); Chunks.RemoveAt(0); } }
Lab.
Make three chunk variants (straight, gap, obstacle row). Confirm the attach point lines up so chunks tile seamlessly with no visible seam or overlap. Object-pool the chunks later instead of destroying for performance.

Player and difficulty

The runner pawn auto-runs forward at a speed that ramps over time; the player only steers between lanes and jumps. Use a FInterpTo to glide between three lane X positions so movement feels smooth, and add a jump via LaunchCharacter. Increase forward speed and obstacle density with distance to create escalating tension.

Design law.
In an endless runner, the player's only job is reaction. Keep the camera stable, telegraph obstacles a full second ahead at current speed, and never spawn an unavoidable pattern.
🎮 Build This: Endless Runner — Lumen Dash

This starts a new genre: a 3D endless runner. It reuses the Character base and Enhanced Input from the 3D foundations lessons but is a new game.

  1. Enable Lumen; add a movable directional light, real-time sky light, and sky atmosphere.
  2. Add an unbound Post Process Volume with manual exposure, subtle bloom, and a vignette.
  3. Build three BP_TrackChunk variants, each with an Attach SceneComponent at the far end.
  4. Write a TrackManager that keeps a rolling window of chunks, spawning ahead and recycling behind.
  5. Make an auto-running pawn with three-lane steering (InterpTo) and a jump (LaunchCharacter).
  6. Ramp forward speed and obstacle density with distance; trigger a bloom/vignette speed-flash on milestones.
  7. Detect obstacle hits to end the run and show distance traveled.

title: UMG UI, HUDs, Menus & a Fighting Game
html:
The interface layer

Every game needs UI: health bars, score, menus, prompts. Unreal's UI framework is UMG (Unreal Motion Graphics) — you design Widget Blueprints visually and bind them to gameplay data. We will build a HUD and menu, then make a fighting game whose health bars, round timer, and combo display are all UMG, with input buffering driving combos.

Widget Blueprints

Create one via Add > User Interface > Widget Blueprint. The editor has two modes: Designer (drag widgets onto a canvas) and Graph (script behavior). The core widget types:

  • Canvas Panel — absolute/anchored layout; the root for HUDs.
  • Horizontal/Vertical Box — auto-flow layout for menus.
  • Text / Image / Button / Progress Bar — the building blocks.
  • Anchors — pin widgets to screen edges so they survive resolution changes.
Tip.
A health bar is just a Progress Bar whose Percent is bound to Health / MaxHealth. Use a Property Binding (the Bind dropdown) for simple values, but prefer an explicit Set Percent call on damage events — bindings tick every frame and add cost.

Showing a widget

Create and add a widget from the PlayerController or HUD class.

FightController.cpp
// HUDClass is a TSubclassOf<UUserWidget> UPROPERTY void AFightController::BeginPlay() { Super::BeginPlay(); if (HUDClass) { HUD = CreateWidget<UUserWidget>(this, HUDClass); HUD->AddToViewport(); } }

Binding data cleanly

The clean pattern: gameplay broadcasts an event, the widget listens. Declare a delegate on the character and let the widget bind to it.

Fighter.h
DECLARE_DYNAMIC_MULTICAST_DELEGATE_OneParam( FOnHealthChanged, float, Pct); UPROPERTY(BlueprintAssignable) FOnHealthChanged OnHealthChanged; void AFighter::TakeHit(float Dmg) { Health = FMath::Max(0.f, Health - Dmg); OnHealthChanged.Broadcast(Health / MaxHealth); }

In the HUD widget's graph, bind OnHealthChanged to a custom event that calls Set Percent on the bar. Now the bar updates only when damage happens.

Menus and game flow

Build a main menu widget with Start, Options, and Quit buttons. OnClicked events call Open Level or Quit Game. For a pause menu, set Set Game Paused(true) and switch input mode to UI Only so the mouse works and gameplay freezes. Remember to set input back to Game Only on resume.

Lab.
Build a pause menu that toggles on a key press, pauses the game, shows a Resume and Quit button, and restores game input on resume. Test that the pawn truly stops moving while paused.

Input buffering for combos

A fighting game can't require frame-perfect presses. Input buffering stores recent inputs with timestamps; when a move's prerequisite completes, you check whether the next input arrived inside a window. Model a combo as an ordered list of move tags and a per-step time budget.

ComboBuffer.cpp
void UComboComponent::PushInput(FName Move) { float Now = GetWorld()->GetTimeSeconds(); // drop inputs older than the buffer window Buffer.RemoveAll([&](const FBufferedInput& I){ return Now - I.Time > BufferWindow; }); Buffer.Add({ Move, Now }); TryAdvanceCombo(); }

On each push, attempt to advance the active combo: if the buffered sequence matches the next expected move within the window, play the corresponding montage and increment the combo counter shown in UMG. Reset the chain on a miss or timeout.

Feel law.
Generous buffers (8 to 12 frames) make combos feel skillful, not lucky. Show the combo count growing in big animated text — UMG Widget Animations can scale-punch the number on each hit.
🎮 Build This: Fighting Game — Knockout Arena

This starts a new genre: a 2.5D fighting game. It reuses the Character + Animation Blueprint work from earlier 3D lessons but is a new game with all-UMG UI.

  1. Build a HUD widget: two health Progress Bars anchored to the screen edges, a centered round timer, and a combo counter.
  2. Add a FOnHealthChanged delegate on the Fighter; bind the bars to it via custom events.
  3. Create a main menu (Start/Quit) and a pause menu that pauses and switches input modes.
  4. Add a ComboComponent with a timestamped input buffer and a buffer window.
  5. Define 2-3 combos as ordered move tags; advance the combo on buffered matches and play montages.
  6. Drive the combo counter text with a UMG scale-punch Widget Animation on each landed hit.
  7. End the round when a fighter's health hits zero; show a victory screen.

title: Behavior Trees, EQS, NavMesh & a Tower-Defense
html:
Thinking enemies

Unreal's AI stack has three pillars: the NavMesh (where things can walk), Behavior Trees (how they decide), and EQS (Environment Query System — where's the best spot to stand/shoot). A tower-defense is the perfect proving ground: enemies path along a route while towers query for targets. We will wire all three.

NavMesh

Drop a NavMeshBoundsVolume and scale it to cover your playable area. Press P in the viewport to visualize the green walkable surface. Anything an AI-controlled Character needs to reach must be inside it and not blocked. Static obstacles carve holes automatically; for runtime changes enable Dynamic navigation generation in Project Settings.

Tip.
If enemies stand still, the usual culprits are: no NavMesh under them, the pawn isn't possessed by an AIController, or there is no path to the goal. Use the Gameplay Debugger (apostrophe key) to inspect the AI's current task and path.

Behavior Trees and Blackboards

A Blackboard is the AI's memory — typed keys like TargetActor (Object), MoveGoal (Vector), HasTarget (Bool). A Behavior Tree reads/writes those keys through nodes:

  • Composites — Selector (try children until one succeeds) and Sequence (run children in order while they succeed).
  • Tasks — leaf actions: Move To, Wait, or your own.
  • Decorators — conditions that gate a branch, e.g. Blackboard: HasTarget is set.
  • Services — run on a tick while a branch is active, e.g. refresh the nearest target.

Run the tree from the AIController:

CreepAIController.cpp
void ACreepAIController::OnPossess(APawn* P) { Super::OnPossess(P); if (BTAsset) { RunBehaviorTree(BTAsset); // set the path goal the creep walks toward Blackboard->SetValueAsVector("MoveGoal", GoalLoc); } }

A custom Behavior Tree task

Built-in tasks cover most needs, but writing one in C++ shows the pattern. A creep that walks waypoints needs a task to fetch the next point.

BTTask_NextWaypoint.cpp
EBTNodeResult::Type UBTTask_NextWaypoint::ExecuteTask( UBehaviorTreeComponent& Comp, uint8* Mem) { auto* C = Cast<ACreep>(Comp.GetAIOwner()->GetPawn()); if (!C || !C->HasNextWaypoint()) return EBTNodeResult::Failed; FVector Next = C->PopWaypoint(); Comp.GetBlackboardComponent() ->SetValueAsVector(GoalKey.SelectedKeyName, Next); return EBTNodeResult::Succeeded; }

The tree then becomes: Sequence → NextWaypoint → MoveTo(MoveGoal) → repeat. When waypoints run out, the creep reaches the base and the player loses a life.

EQS for tower targeting

EQS generates candidate points or actors, scores them by tests, and returns the best. For a tower, query all creeps in range and score by distance to base (prefer the creep closest to your base — the most urgent threat) or by health. Run the query from the tower's Behavior Tree via the Run EQS Query task, or from C++ with UEnvQueryManager::RunEQSQuery. The result feeds the tower's TargetActor blackboard key, which the firing logic reads.

Lab.
Build an EQS query that returns enemy creeps within tower range, scored so the creep nearest the player's base ranks highest. Confirm the tower retargets when a higher-priority creep appears.

Towers, waves, and economy

Towers are placeable actors with a range sphere, a fire rate, and a projectile or hitscan attack. A WaveManager spawns creeps on a timer with growing counts and health. Killing a creep grants currency; the player spends it to place more towers. Track lives; reaching zero ends the run.

Balance law.
Tower-defense difficulty is a curve of enemy DPS-to-reach versus tower DPS-on-path. Tune wave health growth and tower cost together; a tiny change to either swings the whole game.
🎮 Build This: Strategy / Tower-Defense — Creep Crusher

This starts a new genre: a top-down tower-defense built on Unreal's AI stack (NavMesh, Behavior Trees, EQS). It is a new game, not an extension.

  1. Lay out a path with waypoint actors and cover the field with a NavMeshBoundsVolume.
  2. Make a Creep Character + AIController; run a Behavior Tree that walks waypoint to waypoint via a custom NextWaypoint task.
  3. Build a WaveManager that spawns creeps on a timer with rising counts and health.
  4. Create placeable towers with a range sphere, fire rate, and projectile attack.
  5. Use an EQS query to pick each tower's target (creep nearest the base scores highest).
  6. Add currency: killing creeps grants gold; clicking buildable tiles spends it to place towers.
  7. Track lives lost when creeps reach the base; end the game at zero lives.

title: Data Assets, GAS Overview, Inventory & Dialogue for an RPG
html:
Data-driven games

RPGs are content-heavy: dozens of items, abilities, NPCs, and lines of dialogue. Hardcoding that is a nightmare. Unreal's answer is data-driven design — Data Assets and Data Tables hold your content, while code stays generic. We will define items and stats as data, survey the Gameplay Ability System, build an inventory, and script branching dialogue.

Data Assets vs Data Tables

A Primary Data Asset is a class-based content object — subclass UPrimaryDataAsset, add UPROPERTY fields, and create instances in the Content Browser. A Data Table is a spreadsheet of rows sharing one FTableRowBase struct, great for bulk stats. Use Data Assets for rich objects (an item with a mesh, icon, and behavior); use Data Tables for flat tabular data (level XP curves).

ItemData.h
UCLASS() class UItemData : public UPrimaryDataAsset { GENERATED_BODY() public: UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) FText DisplayName; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) UTexture2D* Icon; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) int32 MaxStack = 1; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) int32 Value = 0; };
Tip.
Reference Data Assets by soft pointer (TSoftObjectPtr) when you have hundreds, so they load on demand instead of all at startup. The Asset Manager can stream them by Primary Asset Id.

Gameplay Ability System overview

GAS is Unreal's robust framework for abilities, stats, and status effects — built for networked games. You do not need all of it for a small RPG, but understanding the vocabulary matters:

  • AbilitySystemComponent (ASC) — the hub attached to a pawn that owns abilities and attributes.
  • Gameplay Abilities — activatable actions (a fireball, a dash) with cost and cooldown.
  • Attribute Set — the stats (Health, Mana, Strength) with built-in clamping and replication.
  • Gameplay Effects — data-driven changes to attributes: instant damage, a buff over time, a stun.
  • Gameplay Tags — hierarchical labels (State.Stunned) used everywhere for conditions.
Scope law.
GAS has a steep setup cost. For a solo learning RPG, a simple AttributeComponent with floats may be plenty. Reach for GAS when you need networked, stackable, data-driven effects — then its structure pays off.

Inventory

Model inventory as an array of slots, each holding an item Data Asset reference and a count. Adding an item stacks onto an existing matching slot up to MaxStack, else opens a new slot.

InventoryComponent.cpp
bool UInventoryComponent::AddItem(UItemData* Item, int32 Qty) { for (auto& S : Slots) if (S.Item == Item && S.Count < Item->MaxStack) { S.Count += Qty; OnInventoryChanged.Broadcast(); return true; } Slots.Add({ Item, Qty }); OnInventoryChanged.Broadcast(); return true; }

Bind a UMG inventory grid to OnInventoryChanged (from the last lesson) so the UI rebuilds when items change. Each slot widget shows the item's Icon and count straight from the Data Asset.

Lab.
Define five item Data Assets (potion, sword, key, gold, herb). Make a pickup actor that grants one to the player's InventoryComponent on overlap, and a UMG grid that refreshes on the changed event.

Branching dialogue

Store dialogue as a Data Table of nodes: each row has an Id, the speaker line, and an array of player responses, where each response points to the next node Id (or ends the conversation). A simple state machine walks the table.

DialogueRow.h
USTRUCT() struct FDialogueRow : public FTableRowBase { GENERATED_BODY() UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) FText Line; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) TArray<FText> Choices; UPROPERTY(EditAnywhere) TArray<FName> NextIds; };

The dialogue widget displays Line, builds a button per Choices entry, and on click jumps to the matching NextIds row. An empty NextId closes the dialogue. Gate choices behind inventory or stats for real RPG branching.

🎮 Build This: RPG — Dataforge

This starts a new genre: an action RPG. It reuses the 3D Character/animation base and UMG UI from earlier lessons, but is a new game with data-driven systems.

  1. Define an UItemData Primary Data Asset and create five item instances.
  2. Build an InventoryComponent with stacking slots and an OnInventoryChanged delegate.
  3. Make overlap pickups that grant items; bind a UMG inventory grid to the changed event.
  4. Add an AttributeComponent (Health, Mana, Strength) — or stub GAS if you want the full path.
  5. Store dialogue in a Data Table of FDialogueRow; write a state machine that walks node to node.
  6. Build a dialogue widget: show the line, spawn a button per choice, jump to the next node on click.
  7. Gate at least one dialogue choice behind owning a specific item.

title: Procedural Generation & a Roguelike
html:
Different every run

Roguelikes thrive on procedural generation and permadeath: each run builds a fresh dungeon with randomized loot and enemies, and death sends you back to the start. We will generate dungeon layouts at runtime, populate them with random content drawn from the RPG's Data Assets, and wire a run loop.

Seeded randomness

Reproducibility matters — for sharing seeds and for debugging. Unreal's FRandomStream gives a deterministic generator you control.

DungeonGen.cpp
FRandomStream Rng; void ADungeonGen::Generate(int32 Seed) { Rng.Initialize(Seed); // every Rng call is reproducible for this seed int32 RoomCount = Rng.RandRange(8, 14); for (int32 i = 0; i < RoomCount; ++i) PlaceRoom(); }

Room-based layout

The simplest robust approach is room placement plus corridors. Build rooms as modular Blueprint pieces (floor, four walls with optional door openings). Generate on a grid:

  1. Pick a random grid cell; try to place a room of random size.
  2. Reject if it overlaps an existing room; retry a fixed number of times.
  3. Once placed, connect each new room to the nearest existing room with an L-shaped corridor.
  4. Spawn the actual meshes at the chosen grid transforms.
room placement (C++)
void ADungeonGen::PlaceRoom() { for (int32 tries = 0; tries < 20; ++tries) { FIntRect R = RandomRoomRect(); if (!Overlaps(R)) { Rooms.Add(R); SpawnRoomMesh(R); if (Rooms.Num() > 1) CarveCorridor(Rooms.Last(1).Center(), R.Center()); return; } } }
Tip.
Always carve corridors from each room back to the previously placed room. That guarantees the whole dungeon is a connected graph — no orphaned rooms the player can't reach.

Populating with random content

Reuse the RPG's Data Assets. Keep weighted tables of enemy and item Data Assets; for each non-start room, roll counts and pick entries by weight.

weighted pick (C++)
UItemData* ADungeonGen::RollLoot() { float Total = 0.f; for (auto& E : LootTable) Total += E.Weight; float Pick = Rng.FRandRange(0.f, Total); for (auto& E : LootTable) { Pick -= E.Weight; if (Pick <= 0.f) return E.Item; } return nullptr; }

Place an exit portal in the room farthest from the start (max distance between room centers). Reaching it generates the next floor with a new seed and harder weighting, so difficulty scales with depth.

Lab.
Generate a dungeon, then print the seed on screen. Re-run with the same seed and confirm the layout is identical, then change the seed and confirm it differs. Deterministic generation is the backbone of a fair roguelike.

Permadeath and the run loop

Permadeath means run state lives only for the current run. Keep per-run data (inventory, health, floor number) on the PlayerState or a run object that is discarded on death. On death, show a run summary (floors cleared, gold) and return to a hub or main menu — never reload the same dungeon. Meta-progression (permanent unlocks across runs) is optional and belongs in a SaveGame, which is the next lesson.

Roguelike law.
The thrill comes from meaningful randomness plus permanent stakes. If players can save-scum, tension evaporates; if the randomness is unfair (unwinnable spawns), they feel cheated. Bound your rolls so every run is survivable with skill.
🎮 Build This: Roguelike — Depthforge

This extends the RPG (Dataforge): it reuses that game's item/enemy Data Assets, InventoryComponent, and combat, wrapped in procedural dungeons and a permadeath run loop.

  1. Make a DungeonGen actor using a seeded FRandomStream.
  2. Place non-overlapping rooms on a grid; connect each to the previous with an L-corridor.
  3. Spawn modular room/corridor meshes at the generated transforms.
  4. Populate non-start rooms with weighted enemy and loot rolls from the RPG's Data Assets.
  5. Put an exit portal in the farthest room; entering it generates a harder next floor with a new seed.
  6. Store run state (inventory, health, floor) per-run; on death show a summary and discard it.
  7. Display and allow entering a seed so runs are reproducible.

title: SaveGame, Data Persistence & a Management Sim
html:
State that survives

Until now every game forgot everything on exit. A management sim — building a town, running a farm, managing a colony — needs persistence: the world must be exactly where you left it next session. Unreal's SaveGame system serializes state to disk. We will save and load, then build a small management sandbox on top of it.

The SaveGame object

Subclass USaveGame and add UPROPERTY fields for everything you need to persist. Anything marked UPROPERTY with a savable type serializes automatically.

SimSave.h
USTRUCT() struct FBuildingRecord { GENERATED_BODY() UPROPERTY() FName Type; UPROPERTY() FTransform Xform; UPROPERTY() int32 Level = 1; }; UCLASS() class USimSave : public USaveGame { GENERATED_BODY() public: UPROPERTY() int32 Gold = 0; UPROPERTY() int32 Day = 1; UPROPERTY() TArray<FBuildingRecord> Buildings; };

Writing and reading

Use UGameplayStatics to create, save, and load by slot name. Saving snapshots your live world into the object; loading rebuilds the world from it.

SimGameMode.cpp
void ASimGameMode::SaveAll() { auto* Save = Cast<USimSave>( UGameplayStatics::CreateSaveGameObject(USimSave::StaticClass())); Save->Gold = Gold; Save->Day = Day; for (auto* B : LiveBuildings) Save->Buildings.Add({ B->Type, B->GetActorTransform(), B->Level }); UGameplayStatics::SaveGameToSlot(Save, "Slot0", 0); }
load (C++)
void ASimGameMode::LoadAll() { if (!UGameplayStatics::DoesSaveGameExist("Slot0", 0)) return; auto* Save = Cast<USimSave>( UGameplayStatics::LoadGameFromSlot("Slot0", 0)); Gold = Save->Gold; Day = Save->Day; for (const auto& R : Save->Buildings) SpawnBuilding(R.Type, R.Xform, R.Level); }
Tip.
Save data, not actors. Record what's needed to recreate each object (type, transform, level) and respawn from a Data Asset on load. Never try to serialize live actor pointers — they won't survive a restart.
Pitfall.
Forgetting UPROPERTY() on a field means it silently won't save. If a value resets to default after load, that macro is almost always missing.

Autosave and versioning

Call SaveAll on a timer (every in-game day) and on quit. Add an int32 SaveVersion to your SaveGame so future updates can migrate old saves — check the version on load and patch missing fields rather than rejecting the file.

Lab.
Place a building, save, restart Play-in-Editor, and load. Confirm the building reappears at the exact transform with its level intact. Then bump gold, save, reload, and confirm the number persists.

The simulation loop

A management sim is a clock plus rules. Advance a day timer; each tick, buildings produce or consume resources, the population grows or shrinks, and events fire. Keep the simulation state in plain data so it maps cleanly to your SaveGame. The player's actions — placing buildings, assigning workers, setting taxes — modify the rules, and the sim plays them out over days.

Sim law.
Make the loop legible: show resource deltas per day so players understand cause and effect. A sim where numbers change for invisible reasons feels broken even when the math is correct.
🎮 Build This: Simulation / Management — Tiny Township

This starts a new genre: a build-and-manage simulation. It reuses Data Assets and UMG from earlier lessons but is a new game centered on persistence.

  1. Define building types as Data Assets (cost, production per day, upkeep).
  2. Implement click-to-place buildings on a grid, deducting gold.
  3. Run a day timer; each day, buildings produce/consume resources and update population.
  4. Build a HUD showing gold, day, population, and per-day deltas.
  5. Create a USimSave with gold, day, and a FBuildingRecord array.
  6. Save on quit and on an autosave timer; load on launch, respawning buildings from records.
  7. Add a SaveVersion field and handle a missing field gracefully on load.

title: Replication & Multiplayer Fundamentals
html:
Two players, one world

Multiplayer is where Unreal shines — its networking is battle-tested by shipping titles. The model is client-server: one machine (the server) is authoritative, and clients send input and receive an authoritative copy of the world. We will cover authority, replicated properties, and RPCs, then add basic multiplayer to the FPS from earlier.

The authority model

The server owns the truth. Clients predict and display, but the server decides what actually happened — this is what prevents cheating. Every actor knows its role: GetLocalRole() == ROLE_Authority is true on the server. Gameplay-altering logic (applying damage, spawning pickups) must run on the server.

Golden rule.
Never trust the client. A client can claim anything; only act on it after the server validates. Damage, scoring, and item grants belong on the authority.

Replicated properties

Mark a property to replicate and the server pushes its value to all clients automatically. Add it to GetLifetimeReplicatedProps and set the actor to replicate.

NetCharacter.h / cpp
// .h UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Health) float Health = 100.f; UFUNCTION() void OnRep_Health(); // .cpp void ANetCharacter::GetLifetimeReplicatedProps( TArray<FLifetimeProperty>& Out) const { Super::GetLifetimeReplicatedProps(Out); DOREPLIFETIME(ANetCharacter, Health); }

The ReplicatedUsing (a RepNotify) calls OnRep_Health on clients whenever the value arrives — the place to update the health bar or play a hit reaction. Also set bReplicates = true in the constructor.

Tip.
Replication is server-to-client only. Clients cannot replicate variables up to the server — they must ask via an RPC. And only changes replicate, so a value that's set every frame to the same number won't spam the network.

Remote Procedure Calls

RPCs are functions that execute on a different machine. Three specifiers:

SpecifierRuns onUse
ServerThe server, called by owning clientRequest an action (fire weapon).
ClientOne specific client, called by serverTell a player they were hit.
NetMulticastServer + all clients, called by serverPlay an effect everyone sees.
weapon RPCs (C++)
// .h UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable, WithValidation) void ServerFire(FVector Start, FVector Dir); UFUNCTION(NetMulticast, Unreliable) void MulticastMuzzleFX(); // .cpp bool ANetCharacter::ServerFire_Validate(FVector,FVector){return true;} void ANetCharacter::ServerFire_Implementation( FVector Start, FVector Dir) { // authoritative hitscan here, then: MulticastMuzzleFX(); }

Note Reliable vs Unreliable: reliable RPCs are guaranteed to arrive (use for gameplay events like firing); unreliable may drop (fine for cosmetic effects). WithValidation lets the server reject malformed client requests — your anti-cheat hook.

Lab.
In the editor, set Number of Players to 2 and Net Mode to Play As Listen Server. Move and shoot on one window; confirm the other window sees the movement and the muzzle effect. Replication and RPCs are working when both windows agree.

Movement and possession

Character movement replicates out of the box with built-in client prediction — your locally controlled character feels instant while the server reconciles. The GameMode (server-only) handles login and spawns a pawn per joining player. Keep cosmetic logic on all machines but gameplay decisions on authority, and most of the framework just works.

Pitfall.
Calling a Server RPC from a non-owning client does nothing. RPCs route through ownership: the player must own the actor (their controller possesses it) for client-to-server calls to fire.
🎮 Build This: Multiplayer — FPS Goes Online

This extends the 3D first-person shooter from earlier: take that single-player FPS and make it playable by two clients against a listen server.

  1. Set the FPS character to replicate; replicate Health with a RepNotify that updates the HUD.
  2. Move firing to a Server, Reliable, WithValidation RPC; do authoritative hitscan on the server.
  3. Broadcast muzzle flash and impact via a NetMulticast, Unreliable RPC so all clients see it.
  4. Apply damage only on the server; on death, respawn the player via the GameMode.
  5. Test with 2 players in Play As Listen Server; verify movement, shots, and health sync across windows.
  6. Add a replicated kill counter on PlayerState and show both scores in the HUD.
  7. Guard every gameplay decision with an authority check; keep only effects on clients.
